items.passage: Sea otters use stones as tools to break open shells and reach the soft meat inside. Most of them carry a favorite stone in a loose pouch under their forelimb. When the otter floats on its back, it balances a shell on its chest and strikes the surface with the stone. This simple skill is learned from the mother during the first months of life. Without that careful instruction, young otters often struggle to feed on harder prey.

Invasive plants often outgrow the native species that already share their habitat. They typically arrive with cargo, garden imports, or travelers, and they spread fast once they reach open land. A single weed can cover a roadside within a few seasons by producing many small seeds. Native insects rarely feed on these arrivals, so the plants face little natural control. Land managers now spend much of every summer pulling, mowing, or treating the most aggressive species.

Children pick up new words much faster when the words are tied to a clear context. Studies in classrooms show that learners remember a term better after they see it used during a story or hands-on activity. The brain seems to link the meaning of the word to the small details around it, such as the speaker and the room. Tests with simple flashcards alone produce weaker recall. Teachers therefore plan lessons that mix listening, reading, and short group tasks.

The steam engine quickly transformed work across many British factories. Before this machine, water wheels powered most mills, and the wheels only ran near rivers with reliable flow. With steam, owners could build factories in towns far from any stream. Coal supplied the heat, and iron pipes carried the pressure to giant pistons. Whole industries soon shifted toward the steady output a steam engine could deliver. Workers, however, faced longer shifts and harder rules under the new system.

The deep ocean floor is shaped by powerful forces hidden below the water. Hot magma rises along long underwater ridges and pushes the seabed apart in two directions. Cooled rock spreads sideways and slowly forms wide plains across the basin. Strong currents then carry fine sediment from rivers and dust storms toward these flat areas. Over many centuries, the sediment piles up into thick soft layers. Scientists drill into these layers to study past climate conditions on Earth.

Astronomers now search for distant planets by watching how a star's brightness changes over time. When a planet passes between us and its star, the star dims very slightly for a few hours. Sensitive telescopes record this drop and report it to large computer systems on Earth. The size of the dip helps scientists estimate the diameter of the planet. Repeated transits also reveal how long each year is. Some signals turn out to belong to small rocky worlds.
